yadi māmapratīkāram, aśastraṃ(m) śastrapāṇayaḥ।
dhārtarāṣṭrā raṇe hanyuḥ(s), tanme kṣemataraṃ(m) bhavet॥1.46॥
column4: It would be better for me if the sons of Dhṛtarāṣṭra, armed with weapons, kill me in battle, while I am unarmed and unresisting.
column5: It would be better for me if the sons of Dhṛtarāṣṭra, armed with weapons, kill me in battle, while I am unarmed and unresisting.

column3:
sañjaya uvāca
evamuktvārjunaḥ(s)  saṅkhye,  rathopastha  upāviśat।
visṛjya  saśaraṃ(ñ)  cāpaṃ(m),  śokasaṃvignamānasaḥ॥1.47॥
column4:
Sañjaya said: 
Arjuna, whose mind was agitated by grief on the battlefield, having spoken thus, and having cast aside his bow and arrows, sank into the hinder part of his chariot.
column5:
Sañjaya said: 
Arjuna, whose mind was agitated by grief on the battlefield, having spoken thus, and having cast aside his bow and arrows, sank into the hinder part of his chariot.

column3:
om tatsaditi śrīmadbhagavadgītāsu upaniṣatsu brahmavidyāyāṃ(y̐) yogaśāstre śrīkṛṣṇārjunasaṃvāde arjunaviṣādayogo nāma 
prathamo'dhyāyaḥ ||

|| OM ŚRĪKṚṢṆĀRPAṆAMASTU ||
column4: Thus, in the Upaniṣad sung by the Lord, the Science of Brahma, the scripture of Yoga, the dialogue between Śrī Kṛṣņa and Arjuna, ends the first chapter entitled "The Yoga of Dejection of Arjuna."
column5: Thus, in the Upaniṣad sung by the Lord, the Science of Brahma, the scripture of Yoga, the dialogue between Śrī Kṛṣņa and Arjuna, ends the first chapter entitled "The Yoga of Dejection of Arjuna."

column3:
om śrīparamātmane namaḥ
śrīmadbhagavadgītā
atha dvitīyo'dhyāyaḥ

sañjaya uvāca

taṃ(n) tathā kṛpayāviṣṭam, aśrupūrṇākulekṣaṇam,
viṣīdantamidaṃ( v̐) vākyam, uvāca madhusūdanaḥ॥2.1॥
column4:
Sañjaya said: 
Śrī Kṛṣṇa then addressed the following words to Arjuna, who was as mentioned before, overwhelmed with pity, whose eyes were filled with tears and agitated, and who was full of sorrow.
column5:
Sañjaya said: 
Śrī Kṛṣṇa then addressed the following words to Arjuna, who was as mentioned before, overwhelmed with pity, whose eyes were filled with tears and agitated, and who was full of sorrow.

column3:
sañjaya uvāca

taṃ(n) tathā kṛpayāviṣṭam, aśrupūrṇākulekṣaṇam,
viṣīdantamidaṃ( v̐) vākyam, uvāca madhusūdanaḥ॥2.1॥
column4:
Śrī Bhagavān said: 
Arjuna, how has this infatuation overtaken you at this odd hour? It is shunned by noble souls; neither will it bring heaven, nor fame to you.
column5:
Śrī Bhagavān said: 
Arjuna, how has this infatuation overtaken you at this odd hour? It is shunned by noble souls; neither will it bring heaven, nor fame to you.

column3:
klaibyaṃ(m) mā sma gamaḥ(ph) pārtha, naitattvayyupapadyate,
kṣudraṃ(m) hṛdayadaurbalyaṃ(n), tyaktvottiṣṭha parantapa॥2.3॥
column4: Yield not to unmanliness, Arjuna; it does not befit you. Shaking off this base faint-heartedness stand up , O scorcher of enemies.
column5: Yield not to unmanliness, Arjuna; it does not befit you. Shaking off this base faint-heartedness, stand up, O scorcher of enemies.

column3:
arjuna uvāca

kathaṃ(m) bhīṣmamahaṃ(m) saṅkhye, droṇaṃ(ñ) ca madhusūdana,
iṣubhiḥ(ph) pratiyotsyāmi, pūjārhāvarisūdana॥2.4॥
column4:
Arjuna said: 
How  Kṛṣṇa, shall I fight Bhīṣma and Droṇa with arrows on the battle-field ? They are worthy of deepest reverence, O destroyer of foes.
column5:
Arjuna said: 
O Krishna, how can I fight against Bhishma and Drona with arrows on the battlefield? They are worthy of my deepest reverence, O destroyer of foes.

column3:
gurūnahatvā hi mahānubhāvān,
śreyo bhoktuṃ(m) bhaikṣyamapīha loke,
hatvārthakāmāṃstu gurūnihaiva,
bhuñjīya bhogānrudhirapradigdhān॥2.5॥
column4: It is better to live on alms in this world by not slaying these noble elders, because even after killing them we shall after all enjoy only blood-stained pleasures in the form of wealth and sense-enjoyments.
column5: It is better to live on alms in this world by not slaying these noble elders, because even after killing them we shall after all enjoy only blood-stained pleasures in the form of wealth and sense-enjoyments.

column3:
na caitadvidmaḥ(kh) kataranno garīyo,
yadvā jayema yadi vā no jayeyuḥ।
yāneva hatvā na jijīviṣāmaḥ(s),
te'vasthitāḥ(ph) pramukhe dhārtarāṣṭrāḥ॥2.6॥
column4: We do not even know which is preferable for us– to fight or not to fight; nor do we know whether we shall win or whether they will conquer us. Those very sons of Dhṛtarāṣṭra, killing whom we do not even wish to live, stand in the enemy ranks.
column5: We do not even know which is preferable for us—to fight or not to fight; nor do we know whether we shall win or they will conquer us. Those very sons of Dhṛtarāṣṭra, by killing whom we would not even wish to live, stand in the enemy ranks.

column3:
kārpaṇyadoṣo pahatasvabhāvaḥ(ph),
pṛcchāmi tvāṃ(n) dharmasaṃmūḍhacetāḥ।
yacchreyaḥ(s) syānniścitaṃ(m) brūhi tanme,
śiṣyaste'haṃ(m) śādhi māṃ(n) tvāṃ(m) prapannam॥2.7॥
column4: With my very being smitten by the vice of faint-heartedness and my mind puzzled with regard to duty, I beseech you! tell me that which is decidedly good; I am your disciple. Pray, instruct me, who have taken refuge in you.
column5: With my very being smitten by the vice of faint-heartedness and my mind puzzled with regard to duty, I beseech you! tell me that which is decidedly good; I am your disciple. Pray, instruct me, who have taken refuge in you.

column3:
na hi prapaśyāmi mamāpanudyād,
yacchokamucchoṣaṇamindriyāṇām,
avāpya bhūmāvasapatnamṛddhaṃ(m),
rājyaṃ(m) surāṇāmapi cādhipatyam॥2.8॥
column4: For, even on obtaining undisputed sovereignty and an affluent kingdom on this earth and lordship over the gods, I do not see any means that can drive away the grief which is drying up my senses.
column5: For, even on obtaining undisputed sovereignty and an affluent kingdom on this earth and lordship over the gods, I do not see any means that can drive away the grief which is drying up my senses.

column3:
sañjaya uvāca

evamuktvā hṛṣīkeśaṃ(ṅ), guḍākeśaḥ(ph) parantapa,
na yotsya iti govindam, uktvā tūṣṇīṃ(m) babhūva ha॥2.9॥
column4:
Sañjaya said: 
O King, having thus spoken to Śrī Kṛṣṇa, Arjuna again said to Him,” I will not fight ” and became silent.
column5: O King, having thus spoken to Śrī Kṛṣṇa, Arjuna again said to Him,” I will not fight ” and became silent.

column3:
tamuvāca hṛṣīkeśaḥ(ph), prahasanniva bhārata,
senayorubhayormadhye, viṣīdantamidaṃ(v̐) vacaḥ॥2.10॥
column4: Then, O Dhṛtarāṣṭra, Śrī Kṛṣṇa, as if smiling, addressed the following words to Arjuna, sorrowing in the midst of two armies
column5: Then, O Dhṛtarāṣṭra, Śrī Kṛṣṇa, smiling gently, spoke the following words to Arjuna, who was filled with grief, standing between the two armies.

column3:
śrībhagavānuvāca

aśocyānanvaśocastvaṃ(m), prajñāvādāṃśca bhāṣase,
gatāsūnagatāsūṃśca, nānuśocanti paṇḍitāḥ॥2.11॥
column4:
Śrī Bhagavān said: 
Arjuna, you grieve over those who should not be grieved for and yet speak like the learned; wise men do not sorrow over the dead or the living
column5:
Śrī Bhagavān said: 
Arjuna, you grieve over those who should not be grieved for and yet speak like the learned; wise men do not sorrow over the dead or the living

column3:
na tvevāhaṃ(ñ) jātu nāsaṃ(n), na tvaṃ(n) neme janādhipāḥ,
na caiva na bhaviṣyāmaḥ(s), sarve vayamataḥ(ph) param॥2.12॥
column4: In fact, there was never a time when I was not , or when you or these kings were not. Nor is it a fact that hereafter we shall all cease to be.
column5: Never was there a time when I did not exist, nor you, nor these kings. Nor is it a fact that hereafter we shall all cease to be.

column3:
dehino'sminyathā dehe, kaumāraṃ(y̐) yauvanaṃ(ñ) jarā,
tathā dehāntaraprāptiḥ(r), dhīrastatra na muhyati॥2.13॥
column4: Just as boyhood, youth and old age are attributed to the soul through this body, even so, it attains another body, The wise man does not get deluded about this.
column5: Just as boyhood, youth and old age are attributed to the soul through this body, even so, it attains another body, The wise man does not get deluded about this.

column3:
mātrāsparśāstu kaunteya, śītoṣṇasukhaduḥkhadāḥ,
āgamāpāyino'nityāḥ(s), tāṃstitikṣasva bhārata॥2.14॥
column4: O son of Kuntī, the contacts between the senses and their objects, which give rise to the feeling of heat and cold, pleasure and pain etc., are transitory and fleeting; therefore, Arjuna, endure them.
column5: O son of Kuntī, the contacts between the senses and their objects, which give rise to the feeling of heat and cold, pleasure and pain etc., are transitory and fleeting; therefore, Arjuna, endure them.

column3:
yaṃ(m) hi na vyathayantyete, puruṣaṃ(m) puruṣarṣabha,
samaduḥkhasukhaṃ(n) dhīraṃ(m), so'mṛtatvāya kalpate॥2.15॥
column4: Arjuna, the wise man to whom pain and pleasure are alike, and who is not tormented by these contacts, becomes eligible for immortality
column5: Arjuna, the wise man to whom pain and pleasure are alike, and who is not tormented by these contacts, becomes eligible for immortality

column3:
nāsato vidyate bhāvo, nābhāvo vidyate sataḥ,
ubhayorapi dṛṣṭo'ntaḥ(s), tvanayostattvadarśibhiḥ॥2.16॥
column4: The unreal has no existence, and the real never ceases to be; the reality of both has thus been perceived by the seers of Truth.
column5: The unreal has no existence, and the real never ceases to be; the reality of both has thus been perceived by the seers of Truth

column3:
avināśi tu tadviddhi, yena sarvamidaṃ(n) tatam,
vināśamavyayasyāsya, na kaścitkartumarhati॥2.17॥
column4: Know that alone to be imperishable, which pervades this universe; for no one has power to destroy this indestructible substance.
column5: Know that alone to be imperishable, which pervades this universe; for no one has power to destroy this indestructible substance.

column3:
antavanta ime dehā, nityasyoktāḥ(ś) śarīriṇaḥ,
anāśino'prameyasya, tasmādyudhyasva bhārata॥2.18॥
column4: All these bodies pertaining to the imperishable, indefinable and eternal soul are spoken of as perishable; therefore , Arjuna, fight.
column5: All these bodies pertaining to the imperishable, indefinable and eternal soul are spoken of as perishable; Therefore, Arjuna, fight.

column3:
ya enaṃ(v̐) vetti hantāraṃ,(y̐) yaścainaṃ(m) manyate hatam
ubhau tau na vijānīto, nāyaṃ(m) hanti na hanyate॥2.19॥
column4: Both of them are ignorant, he who considers the soul to be capable of killing and he who takes it as killed; for verily the soul neither kills, nor is killed.
column5: Both of them are ignorant, he who considers the soul to be capable of killing and he who takes it as killed; for verily the soul neither kills, nor is killed.

column3:
na jāyate mriyate vā kadācin,
nāyaṃ(m) bhūtvā bhavitā vā na bhūyaḥ।
ajo nityaḥ(ś) śāśvato'yaṃ(m) purāṇo,
na hanyate hanyamāne śarīre॥2.20॥
column4: The soul is never born, nor it ever dies; nor does it become after being born. For, it is unborn, eternal, everlasting and primeval; even though the body is slain, the soul is not.
column5: The soul is never born, nor it ever dies; nor does it become after being born. For, it is unborn, eternal, everlasting and primeval; even though the body is slain, the soul is not.

column3:
vedāvināśinaṃ(n) nityaṃ(y̐), ya enamajamavyayam,
kathaṃ(m) sa puruṣaḥ(ph) pārtha, kaṃ(ṅ) ghātayati hanti kam॥2.21॥
column4: Arjuna, the man who knows this soul to be imperishable, eternal and free from birth and decay-how and whom will he cause to be killed, how and whom will he kill ?
column5: Arjuna, the man who knows this soul to be imperishable, eternal and free from birth and decay-how and whom will he cause to be killed, how and whom will he kill ?

column3:
vāsāṃsi jīrṇāni yathā vihāya,
navāni gṛhṇāti naro'parāṇi।
tathā śarīrāṇi vihāya jīrṇā-
nyanyāni saṃyāti navāni dehī॥2.22॥
column4: As a man shedding worn-out garments, takes other new ones, likewise, the embodied soul, casting off worn-out bodies, enters into others that are new.
column5: As a man shedding worn-out garments, takes other new ones, likewise, the embodied soul, casting off worn-out bodies, enters into others that are new.

column3:
nainaṃ(ñ) chindanti śastrāṇi, nainaṃ(n) dahati pāvakaḥ,
na cainaṃ(ṅ) kledayantyāpo, na śoṣayati mārutaḥ॥2.23॥
column4: Weapon can not cut it nor can fire burn it; water cannot wet it nor can wind dry it.
column5: Weapons can not cut it nor can fire burn it; water cannot wet it nor can wind dry it.

column3:
acchedyo'ya madāhyo'yam, akledyo'śoṣya eva ca,
nityaḥ(s) sarvagataḥ(s) sthāṇur, acalo'yaṃ(m) sanātanaḥ॥2.24॥
column4: For this soul is incapable of being cut, or burnt by fire; nor can it be desolved by water and is undriable by air as well; This soul is eternal, all-pervading, immovable, constant and everlasting.
column5: For this soul is incapable of being cut, or burnt by fire; nor can it be dissolved by water and cannot be dried by air as well; This soul is eternal, all-pervading, immovable, constant and everlasting.

column3:
avyakto'yama cintyo'yam, avikāryo'yamucyate,
tasmādevaṃ(v̐) viditvainaṃ(n) nānuśocitumarhasi॥2.25॥
column4: This soul is unmanifest; it is incomprehensible and it is spoken of as immutable. Therefore, knowing it as such, you should not grieve.
column5: This soul is unmanifest; it is incomprehensible and it is spoken of as immutable. Therefore, knowing it as such, you should not grieve.

column3:
atha cainaṃ(n) nityajātaṃ(n), nityaṃ(v̐ ) vā manyase mṛtam,
tathāpi tvaṃ(m) mahābāho, naivaṃ śocitumarhasi॥2.26॥
column4: And, Arjuna, if you should assume this soul to be subject to perpetual birth and death, even then you should not grieve like this.
column5: And, Arjuna, if you should assume this soul to be subject to perpetual birth and death, even then you should not grieve like this.

column3:
jātasya hi dhruvo mṛtyuḥ(r), dhruvaṃ(ñ) janma mṛtasya ca,
tasmādaparihārye'rthe, na tvaṃ(m) śocitumarhasi॥2.27॥
column4: For, in that case death is certain for the born, and rebirth is inevitable for the dead.You should not, therefor, grieve over the inevitable.
column5: For, in that case death is certain for the born, and rebirth is inevitable for the dead.You should not, therefore, grieve over the inevitable.

column3:
avyaktādīni bhūtāni, vyaktamadhyāni bhārata,
avyaktanidhanānyeva, tatra kā paridevanā॥2.28॥
column4: Arjuna, before birth beings are not manifest to our human senses; on death they return to the unmanifest again. They are manifest only in the interim between birth and death. What occasion, then, for lamentation?
column5: Arjuna, before birth beings are not manifest to our human senses; on death they return to the unmanifest again. They are manifest only in the interim between birth and death. What occasion, then, for lamentation?

column3:
āścaryavatpaśyati kaścidenam, 
āścaryavadvadati tathaiva cānyaḥ।
āścaryavaccainamanyaḥ(ś) śṛṇoti, 
śrutvā'pyenaṃ(v̐) veda na caiva kaścit॥2.29॥
column4: Hardly any great soul perceives this soul as marvellous, scarce another great soul likewise speaks thereof as marvellous, and scarce another worthy one hears of it as marvellous, while there are some who know it not even on hearing of it.
column5: Hardly any great soul perceives this soul as marvellous, scarce another great soul likewise speaks thereof as marvellous, and scarce another worthy one hears of it as marvellous, while there are some who know it not even on hearing of it.

column3:
dehī nityamavadhyo'yaṃ(n), dehe sarvasya bhārata,
tasmātsarvāṇi bhūtāni, na tvaṃ(m) śocitumarhasi॥2.30॥
column4: Arjuna, this soul dwelling in the bodies of all, can never be slain; therefore , you should not mourn for any one.
column5: Arjuna, this soul dwelling in the bodies of all, can never be slain; therefore , you should not mourn for any one.

column3:
svadharmamapi cāvekṣya, na vikampitumarhasi,
dharmyāddhi yuddhācchreyo'nyat, kṣatriyasya na vidyate॥2.31॥
column4: Besides, considering your own duty too, you should not waver, for there is nothing more welcome for a man of the warrior class than a righteous war.
column5: Besides, considering your own duty too, you should not waver, for there is nothing more welcome for a man of the warrior class than a righteous war.

column3:
yadṛcchayā copapannaṃ(m), svargadvāramapāvṛtam,
sukhinaḥ kṣatriyāḥ(ph) pārtha, labhante yuddhamīdṛśam॥2.32॥
column4: Arjuna, fortunate are the Kṣatriyās who get such an unsolicited opportunity for war, which is an open gateway to heaven.
column5: Arjuna, fortunate are the Kṣatriyās who get such an unsolicited opportunity for war, which is an open gateway to heaven.

column3:
atha cettvamimaṃ(n) dharmyaṃ(m), saṅgrāmaṃ(n) na kariṣyasi,
tataḥ(s) svadharmaṃ(ṅ) kīrtiṃ(ñ) ca, hitvā pāpamavāpsyasi॥2.33॥
column4: Now, if you refuse to fight this righteous war, then, shirking your duty and losing your reputation, you will incur sin.
column5: Now, if you refuse to fight this righteous war, then, shirking your duty and losing your reputation, you will incur sin.

column3:
akīrtiṃ(ñ) cāpi bhūtāni, kathayiṣyanti te'vyayām,
saṃbhāvitasya cākīrtiḥ(r), maraṇādatiricyate॥2.34॥
column4: Nay, people will also pour undying infamy on you; and infamy brought on a man enjoying popular esteem is worse than death.
column5: Nay, people will also pour undying infamy on you; and infamy brought on a man enjoying popular esteem is worse than death

column3:
bhayādraṇāduparataṃ(m), maṃsyante tvāṃ(m) mahārathāḥ,
yeṣāṃ(ñ) ca tvaṃ bahumato, bhūtvā yāsyasi lāghavam॥2.35॥
column4: And the warrior-chiefs who thought highly of you, will now despise you, thinking that it was fear which drove you away from battle.
column5: And the warrior-chiefs who thought highly of you, will now despise you, thinking that it was fear which drove you away from battle

column3:
avācyavādāṃśca bahūn, vadiṣyanti tavāhitāḥ,
nindantastava sāmarthyaṃ(n), tato duḥkhataraṃ(n) nu kim॥2.36॥
column4: And your enemies, disparaging your might, will speak many unbecoming words; what can be more distressing than this?
column5: And your enemies, disparaging your might, will speak many unbecoming words; what can be more distressing than this?

column3:
hato vā prāpsyasi svargaṃ(ñ), jitvā vā bhokṣyase mahīm,
tasmāduttiṣṭha kaunteya, yuddhāya kṛtaniścayaḥ॥2.37॥
column4: Die, and you will win heaven; conquer, and you enjoy sovereignty of the earth; therefore, stand up, Arjuna, determined to fight.
column5: Die, and you will win heaven; conquer, and you enjoy sovereignty of the earth; therefore, stand up, Arjuna, determined to fight.

column3:
sukhaduḥkhe same kṛtvā, lābhālābhau jayājayau,
tato yuddhāya yujyasva, naivaṃ(m) pāpamavāpsyasi॥2.38॥
column4: Treating alike victory and defeat, gain and loss, pleasure and pain, get ready for the battle; fighting thus you will not incur sin.
column5: Treating alike victory and defeat, gain and loss, pleasure and pain, get ready for the battle; fighting thus you will not incur sin.

column3:
eṣā te'bhihitā sāṅkhye, buddhiryoge tvimāṃ(m) śṛṇu,
buddhyā yukto yayā pārtha, karmabandhaṃ(m) prahāsyasi॥2.39॥
column4: Arjuna, this attitude of mind has been presented to you from the point of view of Jñānayoga; now hear the same as presented from the standpoint of Karmayoga (the Yoga of selfless action). Equipped with this attitude of mind, you will be able to throw off completely the shackles of Karma.
column5: Arjuna, this attitude of mind has been presented to you from the point of view of Jñānayoga; now hear the same as presented from the standpoint of Karmayoga (the Yoga of selfless action). Equipped with this attitude of mind, you will be able to throw off completely the shackles of Karma.

column3:
nehābhikramanāśo'sti, pratyavāyo na vidyate,
svalpamapyasya dharmasya, trāyate mahato bhayāt॥2.40॥
column4: In this path (of selfless action) there is no loss of effort, nor is there fear of contrary result, even a little practice of this discipline saves one from the terrible fear of birth and death.
column5: In this path (of selfless action) there is no loss of effort, nor is there fear of contrary result, even a little practice of this discipline saves one from the terrible fear of birth and death

column3:
vyavasāyātmikā buddhiḥ(r), ekeha kurunandana,
bahuśākhā hyanantāśca, buddhayo'vyavasāyinām॥2.41॥
column4: Arjuna, in this Yoga (of selfless action) the intellect is determinate and directed singly towards one ideal; where as the intellect of undecided (ignorant men moved by desires) wanders in all directions, after innumerable aims.
column5: Arjuna, in this Yoga (of selfless action) the intellect is determinate and directed singly towards one ideal; where as the intellect of undecided (ignorant men moved by desires) wanders in all directions, after innumerable aims.

column3:
yāmimāṃ(m) puṣpitāṃ(v̐) vācaṃ(m), pravadantyavipaścitaḥ,
vedavādaratāḥ(ph) pārtha, nānyadastīti vādinaḥ॥2.42॥
column4: Arjuna, those who are full of worldly desires and devoted to the letter of the Vedas, who look upon heaven, as the supreme goal and argue that there is nothing beyond heaven, are unwise.
column5: Arjuna, those who are full of worldly desires and devoted to the letter of the Vedas, who look upon heaven, as the supreme goal and argue that there is nothing beyond heaven, are unwise.

column3:
kāmātmānaḥ(s) svargaparā, janmakarmaphalapradām,
kriyāviśeṣabahulāṃ(m), bhogaiśvaryagatiṃ(m) prati॥2.43॥
column4: They utter flowery speech recommending many rituals of various kinds for the attainment of pleasure and power with rebirth as their fruit.
column5: They utter flowery speech recommending many rituals of various kinds for the attainment of pleasure and power with rebirth as their fruit.

column3:
bhogaiśvaryaprasaktānāṃ(n), tayāpahṛtacetasām,
vyavasāyātmikā buddhiḥ(s), samādhau na vidhīyate॥2.44॥
column4: Those whose minds are carried away by such words, and who are deeply attached to pleasure and worldly power, cannot attain the determinate intellect concentrated on God.
column5: Those whose minds are carried away by such words, and who are deeply attached to pleasure and worldly power, cannot attain the determinate intellect concentrated on God.

column3:
traiguṇyaviṣayā vedā, nistraiguṇyo bhavārjuna,
nirdvandvo nityasattvastho, niryogakṣema ātmavān॥2.45॥
column4: Arjuna, the Vedas thus deal with the evolutes of three Guṇas (modes of Prakṛti), viz., worldly enjoyments and the means of attaining such enjoyments; be thou indifferent to these enjoyments and their means, rising above pairs of opposites like pleasure and pain etc., established in the Eternal Existence (God), absolutely unconcerned about the fulfillments of wants and the preservation of what has been already attained, you be self-controlled.
column5: Arjuna, the Vedas thus deal with the evolutes of three Guṇas (modes of Prakṛti), viz., worldly enjoyments and the means of attaining such enjoyments; be thou indifferent to these enjoyments and their means, rising above pairs of opposites like pleasure and pain etc., established in the Eternal Existence (God), absolutely unconcerned about the fulfillments of wants and the preservation of what has been already attained, you be self-controlled.

column3:
yāvānartha udapāne, sarvataḥ(s) saṃplutodake,
tāvānsarveṣu vedeṣu, brāhmaṇasya vijānataḥ॥2.46॥
column4: A Brāhmaṇa, who has obtained enlightenment, has as much use for all the Vedas as one who stands at the brink of a sheet of water overflowing on all sides has for a small reservoir of water.
column5: A Brāhmaṇa, who has obtained enlightenment, has as much use for all the Vedas as one who stands at the brink of a sheet of water overflowing on all sides has for a small reservoir of water.

column3:
karmaṇyevādhikāraste, mā phaleṣu kadācana,
mā karmaphalaheturbhūr, mā te saṅgo'stvakarmaṇi॥2.47॥
column4: Your right is to work only and never to the fruit thereof. Do not consider yourself to be the cause of the fruit of  actions, nor let your attachment be to inaction.
column5: Your right is to work only and never to the fruit thereof. Do not  be the cause of the fruit of  actions, nor let your attachment be to inaction.

column3:
yogasthaḥ(kh) kuru karmāṇi, sañgaṃ(n) tyaktvā dhanañjaya,
siddhyasiddhyoḥ(s) samo bhūtvā, samatvaṃ(y̐) yoga ucyate॥2.48॥
column4: Arjuna, perform your duties established in Yoga, renouncing attachment, and be even-minded in success and failure; evenness of mind is called "Yoga".
column5: Arjuna, perform your duties established in Yoga, renouncing attachment, and be even-minded in success and failure; evenness of mind is called "Yoga".

column3:
dūreṇa hyavaraṃ(ṅ) karma, buddhiyogāddhanañjaya,
buddhau śaraṇamanviccha, kṛpaṇāḥ(ph) phalahetavaḥ॥2.49॥
column4: Action with a selfish motive is far inferior to this Yoga in the form of equanimity. Do seek refuge in this equipoise of mind, Arjuna; for poor and wretched are those who are the cause in making their actions bear fruits.
column5: Action (with a selfish motive) is far inferior to this Yoga in the form of equanimity. Do seek refuge in this equipoise of mind, Arjuna; for poor and wretched are those who are the cause in making their actions bear fruits.

column3:
buddhiyukto jahātīha, ubhe sukṛtaduṣkṛte,
tasmādyogāya yujyasva, yogaḥ(kh) karmasu kauśalam॥2.50॥
column4: Endowed with equanimity, one sheds in this life both good and evil. Therefor, strive for the practice of this Yoga of equanimity. Skill in action lies in the practice of this Yoga.
column5: Endowed with equanimity, one sheds in this life both good and evil. Therefore, strive for the practice of this Yoga of equanimity. Skill in action lies in (the practice of this) Yoga.

column3:
karmajaṃ(m) buddhiyuktā hi, phalaṃ(n) tyaktvā manīṣiṇaḥ,
janmabandhavinirmuktāḥ(ph), padaṃ(ṅ) gacchantyanāmayam॥2.51॥
column4: For wise men possessing an equipoised mind, renouncing the fruit of actions and freed from the shackles of birth, attain the blissful supreme state.
column5: For wise men possessing an equipoised mind, renouncing the fruit of actions and freed from the shackles of birth, attain the blissful supreme state.

column3:
yadā te mohakalilaṃ(m), buddhirvyatitariṣyati,
tadā gantāsi nirvedaṃ(m), śrotavyasya śrutasya ca॥2.52॥
column4: When your mind will have fully crossed the mire of delusion, you will then grow indifferent to the enjoyments of this world and the next that have been heard of as well as to those that are yet to be heard of.
column5: When your mind will have fully crossed the mire of delusion, you will then grow indifferent to the enjoyments of this world and the next that have been heard of as well as to those that are yet to be heard of.

column3:
śrutivipratipannā te, yadā sthāsyati niścalā,
samādhāvacalā buddhiḥ(s) tadā yogamavāpsyasi॥2.53॥
column4: When your intellect, confused by hearing conflicting statements, will rest steady and undistracted (in meditation) on God, you will then attain Yoga (Everlasting union with God).
column5: When your intellect, confused by hearing conflicting statements, will rest steady and undistracted (in meditation) on God, you will then attain Yoga (Everlasting union with God).

column3:
arjuna uvāca
sthitaprajñasya kā bhāṣā, samādhisthasya keśava,
sthitadhīḥ(kh) kiṃ(m) prabhāṣeta, kimāsīta vrajeta kim॥2.54॥
column4:
Arjuna said: 
Kṛṣṇa, what are the characteristics of a God-realized soul, stable of mind and established in Samādhi (perfect tranquility of mind)? How does the man of stable mind speak, how does he sit, how does he walk?
column5:
Arjuna said: 
Kṛṣṇa, what are the characteristics of a God-realized soul, stable of mind and established in Samādhi (perfect tranquility of mind)? How does the man of stable mind speak, how does he sit, how does he walk?

column3:
śrībhagavānuvāca
prajahāti yadā kāmān, sarvānpārtha manogatān,
ātmanyevātmanā tuṣṭaḥ(s), sthitaprajñastadocyate॥2.55॥
column4:
Śrī Bhagavān said: 
Arjuna, when one thoroughly casts off all cravings of the mind, and is satisfied in the Self through the joy of the Self, then he is called stable of mind.
column5:
Śrī Bhagavān said: 
Arjuna, when one thoroughly casts off all cravings of the mind, and is satisfied in the Self through the (joy of the) Self, then he is called stable of mind.

column3:
duḥkheṣvanudvignamanāḥ(s), sukheṣu vigataspṛhaḥ,
vītarāgabhayakrodhaḥ(s), sthitadhīrmunirucyate॥2.56॥
column4: The sage, whose mind remains unperturbed amid sorrows, whose thirst for pleasures has altogether disappeared, and who is free from passion, fear and anger, is called stable of mind.
column5: He, whose mind remains unperturbed amid sorrows, whose thirst for pleasures has altogether disappeared, and who is free from passion, fear and anger, is called a sage 'stable of mind'.

column3:
yaḥ(s) sarvatrānabhisnehaḥ(s), tattatprāpya śubhāśubham,
nābhinandati na dveṣṭi, tasya prajñā pratiṣṭhitā॥2.57॥
column4: He who is unattached to everything, and meeting with good and evil, neither rejoices nor recoils, his mind is stable.
column5: He who is unattached to everything, and meeting with good and evil, neither rejoices nor recoils, his mind is stable.

column3:
yadā saṃ(nv)harate cāyaṃ(ṅ), kūrmo'ṅgānīva sarvaśaḥ,
indriyāṇīndriyārthe'bhyaḥ(s), tasya prajñā pratiṣṭhitā॥2.58॥
column4: When, like a tortoise, that draws in its limbs from all directions, he withdraws all his senses from the sense-objects, his mind become steady.
column5: When, like a tortoise, that draws in its limbs from all directions, he withdraws all his senses from the sense-objects, his mind becomes steady.

column3:
viṣayā vinivartante, nirāhārasya dehinaḥ,
rasavarjaṃ(m) raso'pyasya, paraṃ(n) dṛṣṭvā nivartate॥2.59॥
column4: Sense-objects turn away from him, who does not enjoy them with his senses; but the taste for them persists, this relish also disappears in the case of the man of stable mind when he realises the Supreme.
column5: Sense-objects turn away from him, who does not enjoy them with his senses; but the taste for them persists, this relish also disappears in the case of the man of stable mind when he realises the Supreme.

column3:
yatato hyapi kaunteya, puruṣasya vipaścitaḥ,
indriyāṇi pramāthīni, haranti prasabhaṃ(m) manaḥ॥2.60॥
column4: Turbulent by nature, the senses (not free from attachment) even of a wise man, who is practicing self-control, forcibly carry away his mind, Arjuna.
column5: Turbulent by nature, the senses even of a wise man, who is practicing self-control, forcibly carry away his mind, Arjuna.

column3:
tāni sarvāṇi saṃyamya, yukta āsīta matparaḥ,
vaśe hi yasyendriyāṇi, tasya prajñā pratiṣṭhitā॥2.61॥
column4: Therefore, having controlled all the senses and concentrating his mind, he should sit for meditation, devoting himself heart and soul to Me. For, he whose senses are under his control, is known to have a stable mind.
column5: Therefore, having controlled all the senses and concentrating his mind, he should sit for meditation, devoting himself heart and soul to Me. For, he whose senses are under his control, is known to have a stable mind.

column3:
dhyāyato viṣayānpuṃsaḥ(s), saṅgasteṣūpajāyate,
saṅgātsañjāyate kāmaḥ(kh), kāmātkrodho'bhijāyate॥2.62॥
column4: The man dwelling on sense-objects develops attachment for them; from attachment springs up desire, and from desire (unfulfilled) ensues anger.
column5: The man dwelling on sense-objects develops attachment for them; from attachment springs up desire, and from desire (unfulfilled) ensues anger.

column3:
krodhādbhavati saṃmohaḥ(s), saṃmohātsmṛtivibhramaḥ,
smṛtibhraṃśād buddhināśo, buddhināśātpraṇaśyati॥2.63॥
column4: From anger arises delusion; from delusion, confusion of memory; from confusion of memory, loss of reason; and from loss of reason one goes to complete ruin.
column5: From anger arises delusion; from delusion, confusion of memory; from confusion of memory, loss of reason; and from loss of reason one goes to complete ruin.

column3:
rāgadveṣaviyuktaistu, viṣayānindriyaiścaran,
ātmavaśyairvidheyātmā, prasādamadhigacchati॥2.64॥
column4: But the self -controlled Sādhaka, while enjoying the various sense-objects through his senses, which are disciplined and free from likes and dislikes, attains placidity of mind.
column5: But the self -controlled Sādhaka, while enjoying the various sense-objects through his senses, which are disciplined and free from likes and dislikes, attains placidity of mind.

column3:
prasāde sarvaduḥkhānāṃ(m), hānirasyopajāyate,
prasannacetaso hyāśu, buddhiḥ(ph) paryavatiṣṭhate॥2.65॥
column4: With the attainment of such placidity of mind, all his sorrows come to an end; and the intellect of such a person of tranquil mind soon withdrawing itself from all sides, becomes firmly established in God.
column5: With the attainment of such placidity of mind, all his sorrows come to an end; and the intellect of such a person of tranquil mind soon withdrawing itself from all sides, becomes firmly established in God.

column3:
nāsti buddhirayuktasya, na cāyuktasya bhāvanā,
na cābhāvayataḥ(ś) śāntiḥ(r) aśāntasya kutaḥ(s) sukham॥2.66॥
column4: He who has not controlled his mind and senses, can have no determinate intellect, nor contemplation. Without contemplation, he can have no peace; and how can there be happiness for one lacking peace of mind?
column5: He who has not controlled his mind and senses, can have no determinate intellect, nor contemplation. Without contemplation, he can have no peace; and how can there be happiness for one lacking peace of mind?

column3:
indriyāṇāṃ(m) hi caratāṃ(y̐), yanmano'nuvidhīyate,
tadasya harati prajñāṃ(v̐), vāyurnāvamivāmbhasi॥2.67॥
column4: As the wind carries away a boat upon the waters, even so, of the senses moving among sense-objects, the one to which the mind is attached, takes away his discrimination.
column5: As the wind carries away a boat upon the waters, even so, of the senses moving among sense-objects, the one to which the mind is attached, takes away his discrimination.

column3:
tasmādyasya mahābāho, nigṛhītāni sarvaśaḥ,
indriyāṇīndriyārthebhyaḥ(s), tasya prajñā pratiṣṭhitā॥2.68॥
column4: Therefore, Arjuna, he, whose senses are completely restrained from their objects, is said to have a stable mind.
column5: Therefore, Arjuna, he, whose senses are completely restrained from their objects, is said to have a stable mind.

column3:
yā niśā sarvabhūtānāṃ(n), tasyāṃ(ñ) jāgarti saṃ(y)yamī,
yasyāṃ(ñ) jāgrati bhūtāni, sā niśā paśyato muneḥ॥2.69॥
column4: That which is night to all beings, in that state of Divine Knowledge and Supreme Bliss the God-realized Yogī keeps awake, and that (the ever-changing, transient worldly happiness) in which all beings keep awake, is night to the seer.
column5: That which is night to all beings, in that state (of Divine Knowledge and Supreme Bliss) the God-realized Yogī keeps awake, and that (the ever-changing, transient worldly happiness) in which all beings keep awake, is night to the seer.

column3:
āpūryamāṇamacalapratiṣṭhaṃ,
samudramāpaḥ(ph) praviśanti yadvat।
tadvatkāmā yaṃ(m) praviśanti sarve,
sa śāntimāpnoti na kāmakāmī॥2.70॥
column4: As the waters of different rivers enter the ocean, which, though full on all sides, remains undisturbed; likewise, he in whom all enjoyments merge themselves without causing disturbance attains peace; not he who hankers after such enjoyments.
column5: As the waters of different rivers enter the ocean, which, though full on all sides, remains undisturbed; likewise, he in whom all enjoyments merge themselves without causing disturbance attains peace; not he who hankers after such enjoyments.

column3:
vihāya kāmānyaḥ(s) sarvān, pumāṃścarati niḥspṛhaḥ,
nirmamo nirahaṅkāraḥ(s), sa śāntimadhigacchati॥2.71॥
column4: He who has given up all desires, and moves free from attachments, egoism and thirst for enjoyment attains peace.
column5: He who has given up all desires, and moves free from attachments, egoism and thirst for enjoyment attains peace.

column3:
eṣā brāhmī sthitiḥ(ph) pārtha, naināṃ(m) prāpya vimuhyati।
sthitvāsyāmantakāle'pi, brahmanirvāṇamṛcchati॥2.72॥
column4: Arjuna, such is the state of God-realized soul; having reached this state, he overcomes delusion. And established in this state, even at the last moment, he attains Brahmic Bliss.
column5: Arjuna, such is the state of God-realized soul; having reached this state, he overcomes delusion. And established in this state, even at the last moment, he attains Brahmic Bliss.

column3:
Om tatsaditi śrīmadbhagavadgītāsu upaniṣatsu 
brahmavidyāyāṃ(y̐) yogaśāstre śrīkṛṣṇārjunasaṃvāde 
sāṅkhyayogo nāma dvitīyo'dhyāyaḥ

|| OM ŚRĪKṚṢṆĀRPAṆAMASTU ||
column4: Thus, in the Upaniṣad sung by the Lord, the Science of Brahma, the scripture of Yoga, the dialogue between Śrī Kṛṣṇa and Arjuna, ends the second chapter entitled ”Sāṅkhyayoga" (The Yoga of Knowledge).
column5: Thus, in the Upaniṣad sung by the Lord, the Science of Brahma, the scripture of Yoga, the dialogue between Śrī Kṛṣṇa and Arjuna, ends the second chapter entitled ”Sāṅkhyayoga" (The Yoga of Knowledge).

column3:
om śrīparamātmane namaḥ
śrīmadbhagavadgītā
atha tṛtīyo'dhyāyaḥ

arjuna uvāca
jyāyasī    cetkarmaṇaste,    matā   buddhirjanārdana।
tatkiṃ(ṅ) karmaṇi ghore māṃ(n), niyojayasi keśava॥3.1॥
column4: Arjuna said :Kṛṣņa, if You consider Knowledge as superior to Action, why then do You urge me to this dreadful action, Keśava!
column5:
Arjuna said : 
Kṛṣņa, if You consider Knowledge as superior to Action, why then do You urge me to this dreadful action, Keśava!

column3:
vyāmiśreṇeva  vākyena,  buddhiṃ(m)  mohayasīva  me।
tadekaṃ(v̐) vada niścitya, yena śreyo'hamāpnuyām॥3.2॥
column4: You are, as it were, puzzling my mind by these seemingly conflicting expressions; therefore, tell me the one definite discipline by which I may obtain the highest good.
column5: You are, as it were, puzzling my mind by these seemingly conflicting expressions; therefore, tell me the one definite discipline by which I may obtain the highest good.

column3:
śrībhagavānuvāca
loke'smindvividhā  niṣṭhā,  purā  proktā  mayānagha।
jñānayogena  sāṅkhyānāṃ(ṅ),  karmayogena  yoginām॥3.3॥
column4: Śrī Bhagavān said:Arjuna, in this world two courses of Sādhanā (spiritual discipline) have been enunciated by Me in the past. In the case of the Sāṅkhyayogī, the Sādhanā proceeds along the path of Knowledge; whereas in the case of the Karmayogī, it proceeds along the path of Action.
column5:
Śrī Bhagavān said: 
Arjuna, in this world two courses of Sādhanā (spiritual discipline) have been enunciated by Me in the past. In the case of the Sāṅkhyayogī, the Sādhanā proceeds along the path of Knowledge; whereas in the case of the Karmayogī, it proceeds along the path of Action.

column3:
na karmaṇāmanārambhān, naiṣkarmyaṃ(m) puruṣo'śnute।
na ca sannyasanādeva, siddhiṃ(m) samadhigacchati॥3.4॥
column4: Man does not attain freedom from action (culmination of the discipline of Action) without entering upon action; nor does he reach perfection (culmination of the discipline of Knowledge) merely by ceasing to act.
column5: Man does not attain freedom from action (culmination of the discipline of Action) without entering upon action; nor does he reach perfection (culmination of the discipline of Knowledge) merely by ceasing to act.

column3:
na     hi    kaścitkṣaṇamapi,    jātu   tiṣṭhatyakarmakṛt।
kāryate hyavaśaḥ(kh) karma, sarvaḥ(ph) prakṛtijairguṇaiḥ॥3.5॥
column4: Surely, none can ever remain inactive even for a moment; for, everyone is helplessly driven to action by modes of Prakṛti (nature born qualities).
column5: Surely, none can ever remain inactive even for a moment; for, everyone is helplessly driven to action by modes of Prakṛti (nature born qualities).

column3:
karmendriyāṇi  saṃyamya,  ya   āste  manasā  smaran।
indriyārthānvimūḍhātmā,   mithyācāraḥ(s)   sa   ucyate॥3.6॥
column4: He who outwardly restraining the organs of sense and action, sits mentally dwelling on the objects of senses, that man of deluded intellect is called a hypocrite.
column5: He who outwardly restraining the organs of sense and action, sits mentally dwelling on the objects of senses, that man of deluded intellect is called a hypocrite.

column3:
yastvindriyāṇi      manasā,    niyamyārabhate'rjuna।
karmendriyaiḥ(kh) karmayogam, asaktaḥ(s) sa viśiṣyate॥3.7॥
column4: On the other hand, he who controlling the organs of sense and action by the power of his will, and remaining unattached, undertakes the Yoga of selfless Action through those organs, Arjuna, he excels.
column5: On the other hand, he who controlling the organs of sense and action by the power of his will, and remaining unattached, undertakes the Yoga of selfless Action through those organs, Arjuna, he excels.

column3:
niyataṃ(ṅ) kuru karma tvaṃ(ṅ), karma jyāyo hyakarmaṇaḥ।
śarīrayātrāpi    ca   te,   na   prasiddhyedakarmaṇaḥ॥3.8॥
column4: Therefore, do you perform your allotted duty; for action is superior to inaction. Desisting from action, you cannot even maintain your body.
column5: Therefore, do you perform your allotted duty; for action is superior to inaction. Desisting from action, you cannot even maintain your body.

column3:
yajñārthātkarmaṇo'nyatra, loko'yaṃ(ṅ) karmabandhanaḥ।
tadarthaṃ(ṅ) karma kaunteya, muktasaṅgaḥ(s) samācara॥3.9॥
column4: Man is bound by his own action except when it is performed for the sake of sacrifice. Therefore, Arjuna, do you efficiently perform your duty, free from attachment, for the sake of sacrifice alone.
column5: Man is bound by his own action except when it is performed for the sake of sacrifice. Therefore, Arjuna, do you efficiently perform your duty, free from attachment, for the sake of sacrifice alone.

column3:
sahayajñāḥ(ph)  prajāḥ(s)  sṛṣṭvā,  purovāca  prajāpatiḥ।
anena   prasaviṣyadhvam,   eṣa    vo'stviṣṭakāmadhuk॥3.10॥
column4: Having created mankind along with Yajña, at the beginning of creation, the creator, Brahmā, said to them, "You shall prosper by this; may this yield the enjoyments you seek.
column5: Having created mankind along with Yajña, at the beginning of creation, the creator, Brahmā, said to them, "You shall prosper by this; may this yield the enjoyments you seek.

column3:
devānbhāvayatānena,   te    devā  bhāvayantu   vaḥ।
parasparaṃ(m) bhāvayantaḥ(ś), śreyaḥ(ph) paramavāpsyatha॥3.11॥
column4: Foster the gods through this sacrifice, and let the gods foster you. Thus, each fostering the other selflessly, you will attain the highest good.
column5: Foster the gods through this sacrifice, and let the gods foster you. Thus, each fostering the other selflessly, you will attain the highest good..

column3:
iṣṭānbhogānhi  vo  devā,   dāsyante  yajñabhāvitāḥ।
tairdattānapradāyaibhyo,  yo  bhuṅkte  stena  eva saḥ॥3.12॥
column4: Fostered by sacrifice, the gods will surely bestow on you unasked all the desired enjoyments. He who enjoys the gifts bestowed by them without offering their share to them, is undoubtedly a thief.
column5: Fostered by sacrifice, the gods will surely bestow on you unasked all the desired enjoyments. He who enjoys the gifts bestowed by them without offering their share to them, is undoubtedly a thief.

column3:
yajñaśiṣṭāśinaḥ(s)  santo,  mucyante  sarvakilbiṣaiḥ।
bhuñjate te tvaghaṃ(m) pāpā, ye pacantyātmakāraṇāt॥3.13॥
column4: The virtuous who partake of what is left over after sacrifice, are absolved of all sins. Those sinful ones who cook for the sake of nourishing their bodies alone, partake of sin only.
column5: The virtuous who partake of what is left over after sacrifice, are absolved of all sins. Those sinful ones who cook for the sake of nourishing their bodies alone, partake of sin only.

column3:
annādbhavanti   bhūtāni,   parjanyādannasambhavaḥ।
yajñādbhavati parjanyo, yajñaḥ(kh) karmasamudbhavaḥ॥3.14॥
column4: All beings are evolved from food; production of food is dependent on rain; rain ensues from sacrifice, and sacrifice is rooted in prescribed action.
column5: All beings are evolved from food; production of food is dependent on rain; rain ensues from sacrifice, and sacrifice is rooted in prescribed action.

column3:
karma brahmodbhavaṃ(v̐) viddhi, brahmākṣarasamudbhavam।
tasmātsarvagataṃ(m) brahma, nityaṃ(y̐) yajñe pratiṣṭhitam॥3.15॥
column4: Know that prescribed action has its origin in the Vedas, and the Vedas proceed from the Indestructible (God); hence the all-pervading Infinite is always present in sacrifice.
column5: Know that prescribed action has its origin in the Vedas, and the Vedas proceed from the Indestructible (God); hence the all-pervading Infinite is always present in sacrifice

column3:
evaṃ(m) pravartitaṃ(ñ) cakraṃ(n), nānuvartayatīha yaḥ।
aghāyurindriyārāmo,  moghaṃ(m)  pārtha  sa  jīvati॥3.16॥
column4: Arjuna, he who does not follow the wheel of creation thus set going in this world i.e., does not perform his duties, leads a sinful and sensual life, he lives in vain.
column5: Arjuna, he who does not follow the wheel of creation thus set going in this world i.e., does not perform his duties, leads a sinful and sensual life, he lives in vain

column3:
yastvātmaratireva  syād,  ātmatṛptaśca  mānavaḥ।
ātmanyeva ca santuṣṭaḥ(s), tasya kāryaṃ(n) na vidyate॥3.17॥
column4: He, however, who takes delight in the Self alone and is gratified with the Self, and is contented in the Self, has no duty.
column5: He, however, who takes delight in the Self alone and is gratified with the Self, and is contented in the Self, has no duty.

column3:
naiva    tasya    kṛtenārtho,    nākṛteneha   kaścana।
na   cāsya   sarvabhūteṣu,  kaścidarthavyapāśrayaḥ॥3.18॥
column4: In this world that great soul has nothing to gain by action nor by abstaining from action; nor has he selfish dependence of any kind on any creature.
column5: In this world that great soul has nothing to gain by action nor by abstaining from action; nor has he selfish dependence of any kind on any creature.

column3:
tasmādasaktaḥ(s) satataṃ(ṅ), kāryaṃ(ṅ) karma samācara।
asakto   hyācarankarma,  paramāpnoti   pūruṣaḥ॥3.19॥
column4: Therefore, go on efficiently doing your duty at all times without attachment. Doing work without attachment man attains the Supreme.
column5: Therefore, go on efficiently doing your duty at all times without attachment. Doing work without attachment man attains the Supreme.

column3:
karmaṇaiva   hi   saṃsiddhim,   āsthitā    janakādayaḥ।
lokasaṅgrahamevāpi,      sampaśyankartumarhasi॥3.20॥
column4: It is through action without attachment alone that Janaka and other wise men reached perfection. Having in view the maintenance of the world order too, you should take to action.
column5: It is through action without attachment alone that Janaka and other wise men reached perfection. Having in view the maintenance of the world order too, you should take to action.

column3:
yadyadācarati    śreṣṭhaḥ(s),   tattadevetaro janaḥ।
sa   yatpramāṇaṃ(ṅ)     kurute,    lokastadanuvartate॥3.21॥
column4: For, whatever a great man does, that very thing other men also do; whatever standard he sets up, the generality of men follow the same.
column5: For, whatever a great man does, that very thing other men also do; whatever standard he sets up, the generality of men follow the same.

column3:
na  me  pārthāsti  kartavyaṃ(n),  triṣu  lokeṣu  kiñcana।
nānavāptamavāptavyaṃ(v̐),  varta  eva ca  karmaṇi॥3.22॥
column4: Arjuna, there is no duty in all the three worlds for Me to perform, nor is there anything worth attaining, unattained by Me; yet I continue to work.
column5: Arjuna, there is no duty in all the three worlds for Me to perform, nor is there anything worth attaining, unattained by Me; yet I continue to work.

column3:
yadi hyahaṃ(n) na varteyaṃ(ñ), jātu karmaṇyatandritaḥ।
mama vartmānuvartante, manuṣyāḥ(ph) pārtha sarvaśaḥ॥3.23॥
column4: Should I not engage in action scrupulously at any time, great harm will come to the world; for, Arjuna, men follow My way in all matters.
column5: Should I not engage in action scrupulously at any time, great harm will come to the world; for, Arjuna, men follow My way in all matters.

column3:
utsīdeyurime   lokā,   na   kuryāṃ(ṅ)   karma   cedaham।
saṅkarasya ca kartā syām, upahanyāmimāḥ(ph) prajāḥ॥3.24॥
column4: If I ever cease to act, these worlds would perish; nay, I should prove to be the cause of confusion, and of the destruction of these people.
column5: If I ever cease to act, these worlds would perish; nay, I should prove to be the cause of confusion, and of the destruction of these people.

column3:
saktāḥ(kh) karmaṇyavidvāṃso, yathā kurvanti bhārata।
kuryādvidvāṃstathāsaktaḥ(ś),   cikīrṣurlokasaṅgraham॥3.25॥
column4: Arjuna, as the unwise act with attachment, so should the wise man, with a view to maintain the world order, act without attachment.
column5: Arjuna, as the unwise act with attachment, so should the wise man, with a view to maintain the world order, act without attachment.

column3:
na buddhibhedaṃ(ñ) janayed, ajñānāṃ(ṅ) karmasaṅginām।
joṣayetsarvakarmāṇi,   vidvānyuktaḥ(s)   samācaran॥3.26॥
column4: A wise man established in the Self should not unsettle the mind of the ignorant attached to action, but should get them to perform all their duties, duly performing his own duties.
column5: A wise man established in the Self should not unsettle the mind of the ignorant attached to action, but should get them to perform all their duties, duly performing his own duties.

column3:
prakṛteḥ(kh) kriyamāṇāni,  guṇaiḥ(kh) karmāṇi sarvaśaḥ।
ahaṅkāravimūḍhātmā,      kartāhamiti     manyate॥3.27॥
column4: In fact all actions are being performed by the modes of Prakṛti (Primordial Nature). The fool, whose mind is deluded by egoism, thinks: "I am the doer.”
column5: In fact all actions are being performed by the modes of Prakṛti (Primordial Nature). The fool, whose mind is deluded by egoism, thinks: "I am the doer.”

column3:
tattvavittu     mahābāho,    guṇakarmavibhāgayoḥ।
guṇā    guṇeṣu    vartanta,    iti    matvā   na   sajjate॥3.28॥
column4: However, he who has true insight into the respective spheres of Guņas (modes of Prakṛti) and their actions, holding that it is the Guņas (in the form of the senses, mind, etc.,) that move among the Guņas (objects of perception), does not get attached to them, Arjuna.
column5: However, he who has true insight into the respective spheres of Guņas (modes of Prakṛti) and their actions, holding that it is the Guņas (in the form of the senses, mind, etc.,) that move among the Guņas (objects of perception), does not get attached to them, Arjuna.

column3:
prakṛterguṇasammūḍhāḥ(s),   sajjante   guṇakarmasu।
tānakṛtsnavido   mandān,   kṛtsnavinna   vicālayet॥3.29॥
column4: Those who are completely deluded by the Guņas (modes) of Prakṛti remain attached to those Guņas and actions; the man of perfect Knowledge should not unsettle the mind of those ignorants of imperfect knowledge.
column5: Those who are completely deluded by the Guņas (modes) of Prakṛti remain attached to those Guņas and actions; the man of perfect Knowledge should not unsettle the mind of those ignorants of imperfect knowledge.

column3:
mayi  sarvāṇi  karmāṇi,  sannyasyādhyātmacetasā।
nirāśīrnirmamo    bhūtvā,    yudhyasva    vigatajvaraḥ॥3.30॥
column4: Renouncing all actions in Me, with the mind centred in the Self, free from hope and egoism, and from (mental) fever, engage in battle.
column5: Renouncing all actions in Me, with the mind centred in the Self, free from hope and egoism, and from (mental) fever, engage in battle.

column3:
ye me  matamidaṃ(n)  nityam,   anutiṣṭhanti  mānavāḥ।
śraddhāvanto'nasūyanto,   mucyante   te'pi   karmabhiḥ॥3.31॥
column4: Even those men who, with an uncavilling and devout mind, always follow this teaching of Mine, are released from the bondage of all actions.
column5: Even those men who, with an uncavilling and devout mind, always follow this teaching of Mine, are released from the bondage of all actions.

column3:
ye  tvetadabhyasūyanto,  nānutiṣṭhanti  me  matam।
sarvajñānavimūḍhāṃstān,    viddhi   naṣṭānacetasaḥ॥3.32॥
column4: But they, however, who, finding fault with this teaching of Mine, do not follow it, take those fools to be deluded in the matter of all knowledge as lost.
column5: But they, however, who, finding fault with this teaching of Mine, do not follow it, take those fools to be deluded in the matter of all knowledge as lost.

column3:
sadṛśaṃ(ñ)   ceṣṭate   svasyāḥ(ph),   prakṛterjñānavānapi।
prakṛtiṃ(y̐) yānti bhūtāni, nigrahaḥ(kh) kiṃ(ṅ) kariṣyati॥3.33॥
column4: All living creatures follow their tendencies; even the wise man acts according to the tendencies of his own nature. Of what use is restraint by force.
column5: All living creatures follow their tendencies; even the wise man acts according to the tendencies of his own nature. Of what use is restraint by force.

column3:
indriyasyendriyasyārthe,   rāgadveṣau   vyavasthitau।
tayorna  vaśamāgacchet,  tau  hyasya  paripanthinau॥3.34॥
column4: Attraction and repulsion are rooted in all sense-objects. Man should never allow himself to be swayed by them, because they are the two principal enemies standing in the way of his redemption.
column5: Attraction and repulsion are rooted in all sense-objects. Man should never allow himself to be swayed by them, because they are the two principal enemies standing in the way of his redemption.

column3:
śreyānsvadharmo viguṇaḥ(ph), paradharmātsvanuṣṭhitāt।
svadharme nidhanaṃ(m) śreyaḥ(ph), paradharmo bhayāvahaḥ॥3.35॥
column4: One's own duty, though devoid of merit, is preferable to the duty of another well performed. Even death in the performance of one's own duty brings blessedness; another's duty is fraught with fear.
column5: One's own duty, though devoid of merit, is preferable to the duty of another well performed. Even death in the performance of one's own duty brings blessedness; another's duty is fraught with fear.

column3:
arjuna uvāca
atha  kena  prayukto'yaṃ(m),  pāpaṃ(ñ)  carati  pūruṣaḥ।
anicchannapi     vārṣṇeya,    balādiva    niyojitaḥ॥3.36॥
column4: Arjuna said :Now impelled by what, Kṛṣņa, does this man commit sin even involuntarily, as though driven by force?
column5:
Arjuna said : 
Now impelled by what, Kṛṣņa, does this man commit sin even involuntarily, as though driven by force?

column3:
śrībhagavānuvāca
kāma   eṣa   krodha   eṣa,    rajoguṇasamudbhavaḥ।
mahāśano  mahāpāpmā,  viddhyenamiha  vairiṇam॥3.37॥
column4: Śrī Bhagavān said :It is desire begotten of the element of Rajas, which appears as wrath; nay, it is insatiable and grossly wicked. Know this to be the enemy in this case.
column5:
Śrī Bhagavān said : 
It is desire begotten of the element of Rajas, which appears as wrath; nay, it is insatiable and grossly wicked. Know this to be the enemy in this case.

column3:
dhūmenāvriyate  vahniḥ(r),  yathādarśo  malena  ca।
yatholbenāvṛto  garbhaḥ(s),  tathā  tenedamāvṛtam॥3.38॥
column4: As fire is covered by smoke, mirror by dust, and embryo by the amnion, so is knowledge covered by desire.
column5: As fire is covered by smoke, mirror by dust, and embryo by the amnion, so is knowledge covered by desire.

column3:
āvṛtaṃ(ñ)    jñānametena,   jñānino   nityavairiṇā।
kāmarūpeṇa     kaunteya,     duṣpūreṇānalena   ca॥3.39॥
column4: And, Arjuna, Knowledge stands covered by this eternal enemy of the wise, known as desire, which is insatiable like fire.
column5: And, Arjuna, Knowledge stands covered by this eternal enemy of the wise, known as desire, which is insatiable like fire.

column3:
indriyāṇi   mano  buddhiḥ(r),  asyādhiṣṭhānamucyate।
etairvimohayatyeṣa,     jñānamāvṛtya    dehinam॥3.40॥
column4: The senses, the mind and the intellect are declared to be its seat; covering the knowledge through these, it (desire) deludes the embodied soul.
column5: The senses, the mind and the intellect are declared to be its seat; covering the knowledge through these, it (desire) deludes the embodied soul.

column3:
tasmāttvamindriyāṇyādau,  niyamya  bharatarṣabha।
pāpmānaṃ(m) prajahi hyenaṃ(ñ), jñānavijñānanāśanam॥3.41॥
column4: Therefore, Arjuna, you must first control your senses, and then kill this evil thing which obstructs Jñāna (Knowledge of the Absolute or Nirguņa Brahma) and Vijñāna (Knowledge of Sākāra Brahma or manifest Divinity).
column5: Therefore, Arjuna, you must first control your senses, and then kill this evil thing which obstructs Jñāna (Knowledge of the Absolute or Nirguņa Brahma) and Vijñāna (Knowledge of Sākāra Brahma or manifest Divinity).

column3:
indriyāṇi parāṇyāhuḥ(r), indriyebhyaḥ(ph) paraṃ(m) manaḥ।
manasastu parā buddhiḥ(r), yo buddheḥ(ph) paratastu saḥ॥3.42॥
column4: The senses are said to be greater than the body; but greater than the senses is the mind. Greater than the mind is the intellect; and what is greater than the intellect is He, the Self.
column5: The senses are said to be greater than the body; but greater than the senses is the mind. Greater than the mind is the intellect; and what is greater than the intellect is He, the Self.

column3:
evaṃ(m) buddheḥ(ph) paraṃ(m) buddhvā, saṃstabhyātmānamātmanā।
jahi   śatruṃ(m)   mahābāho,   kāmarūpaṃ(n)   durāsadam॥3.43॥
column4: Thus, Arjuna, knowing the Self which is higher than the intellect and subduing the mind by reason, kill this enemy in the form of desire that is hard to overcome.
column5: Thus, Arjuna, knowing the Self which is higher than the intellect and subduing the mind by reason, kill this enemy in the form of desire that is hard to overcome.

column3:
Om tatsaditi śrīmadbhagavadgītāsu upaniṣatsu brahmavidyāyāṃ(y̐)
 yogaśāstre śrīkṛṣṇārjunasaṃvāde karmayogo nāma 
tṛtīyo'dhyāyaḥ ||

|| OM ŚRĪKṚṢṆĀRPAṆAMASTU ||

om śrīparamātmane namaḥ
Śrīmadbhagavadgītā
atha caturtho'dhyāyaḥ

śrībhagavānuvāca
imaṃ(v̐)  vivasvate  yogaṃ(m),   proktavānahamavyayam।
vivasvānmanave   prāha,   manurikṣvākave'bravīt॥4.1॥
column4: Śrī bhagvān said: I revealed this imortal yoga to Vivasvān(sun-god); Vivasvān conveyd it to Manu(his son); and Manu Imparted it to his son Ikṣvāku.
column5:
Śrī Bhagavān said: 
I revealed this immortal yoga to Vivasvān(sun-god); Vivasvān conveyed it to Manu(his son); and Manu Imparted it to his son Ikṣvāku.

column3:
evaṃ(m) paramparāprāptam,  imaṃ(m) rājarṣayo viduḥ।
sa kāleneha  mahatā,  yogo   naṣṭaḥ(ph)  parantapa॥4.2॥
column4: Thus transmitted in succession from father to son, Arjuna, this Yoga remained known to the Rājaraṣis is (royal sages). Through long lapse of time, this Yoga got lost to the world.
column5: Thus transmitted in succession from father to son, Arjuna, this Yoga remained known to the Rājaraṣis (royal sages). Through long lapse of time, this Yoga got lost to the world.

column3:
sa evāyaṃ(m) mayā te'dya, yogaḥ(ph) proktaḥ(ph) purātanaḥ।
bhakto'si me  sakhā  ceti,   rahasyaṃ(m)   hyetaduttamam॥4.3॥
column4: The same ancient Yoga, which is the supreme secret, has this day been imparted to you by Me, because you are My devotee and friend.
column5: The same ancient Yoga, which is the supreme secret, has this day been imparted to you by Me, because you are My devotee and friend.

column3:
arjuna uvāca
aparaṃ(m) bhavato janma, paraṃ(ñ) janma vivasvataḥ।
kathametadvijānīyāṃ(n),   tvamādau   proktavāniti॥4.4॥
column4: Arjuna said: You are of recent origin, while the birth of Vivāsvan dates back to remote antiquity. How, then, am I to believe that You imparted this Yoga at the beginning of the creation?
column5:
Arjuna said: 
You are of recent origin, while the birth of Vivāsvan dates back to remote antiquity. How, then, am I to believe that You imparted this Yoga at the beginning of the creation?

column3:
śrībhagavānuvāca
bahūni   me    vyatītāni,     janmāni    tava   cārjuna।
tānyahaṃ(v̐)   veda   sarvāṇi,   na   tvaṃ(v̐)   vettha  parantapa॥4.5॥
column4: Śrī Bhagavan said : Arjuna, you and I have passed through many births; I remember them all; you do not remember, O chastiser of foes.
column5:
Śrī Bhagavan said : 
Arjuna, you and I have passed through many births; I remember them all; you do not remember, O chastiser of foes.

column3:
ajo'pi   sannavyayātmā,   bhūtānāmīśvaro'pi   san।
prakṛtiṃ(m) svāmadhiṣṭhāya, sambhavāmyātmamāyayā॥4.6॥
column4: Though birthless and immortal and the Lord of all beings, I manifest Myself through My own Yogamāyā (divine potency), keeping My nature (Prakṛti) under control.
column5: Though birthless and immortal and the Lord of all beings, I manifest Myself through My own Yogamāyā (divine potency), keeping My nature (Prakṛti) under control.

column3:
yadā  yadā  hi  dharmasya,   glānirbhavati  bhārata।
abhyutthānamadharmasya, tadātmānaṃ(m) sṛjāmyaham॥4.7॥
column4: Arjuna, whenever righteousness is on the decline, unrighteousness is in the ascendant, then I body Myself forth.
column5: Arjuna, whenever righteousness is on the decline, unrighteousness is in the ascendant, then I body Myself forth.

column3:
paritrāṇāya   sādhūnāṃ(v̐),   vināśāya  ca  duṣkṛtām।
dharmasaṃsthāpanārthāya,  sambhavāmi  yuge  yuge॥4.8॥
column4: For the protection of the virtuous, for the extirpation of evil-doers, and for establishing Dharma (righteousness) on a firm footing, I manifest Myself from age to age.
column5: For the protection of the virtuous, for the extirpation of evil-doers, and for establishing Dharma (righteousness) on a firm footing, I manifest Myself from age to age.

column3:
janma karma  ca  me  divyam,  evaṃ(y̐)  yo  vetti  tattvataḥ।
tyaktvā dehaṃ(m) punarjanma, naiti māmeti so'rjuna॥4.9॥
column4: Arjuna, My birth and activities are divine. He who knows this in reality is not reborn on leaving his body, but comes to Me.
column5: Arjuna, My birth and activities are divine. He who knows this in reality is not reborn on leaving his body, but comes to Me.

column3:
vītarāgabhayakrodhā,  manmayā  māmupāśritāḥ।
bahavo   jñānatapasā,      pūtā      madbhāvamāgatāḥ॥4.10॥
column4: Completely rid of attachment, fear and anger, wholly absorbed in Me, depending on Me, and purified by the penance of wisdom, many have become one with Me even in the past.
column5: Completely rid of attachment, fear and anger, wholly absorbed in Me, depending on Me, and purified by the penance of wisdom, many have become one with Me even in the past.

column3:
ye yathā māṃ(m) prapadyante, tāṃstathaiva bhajāmyaham।
mama vartmānuvartante, manuṣyāḥ(ph) pārtha sarvaśaḥ॥4.11॥
column4: Completely rid of attachment, fear and anger, wholly absorbed in Me, depending on Me, and purified by the penance of wisdom, many have become one with Me even in the past.
column5: Arjuna, howsoever men seek Me, even so do I respond to them; for all men follow My path in everyway.

column3:
kāṅkṣantaḥ(kh) karmaṇāṃ(m) siddhiṃ(y̐), yajanta iha devatāḥ।
kṣipraṃ(m) hi mānuṣe loke, siddhirbhavati karmajā॥4.12॥
column4: In this world of human beings, men seeking the fruition of their activities, worship the gods; for success born of actions follows quickly.
column5: In this world of human beings, men seeking the fruition of their activities, worship the gods; for success born of actions follows quickly.

column3:
cāturvarṇyaṃ(m) mayā sṛṣṭaṃ(ṅ), guṇakarmavibhāgaśaḥ।
tasya  kartāramapi  māṃ(v̐),  viddhyakartāramavyayam॥4.13॥
column4: The four orders of society (viz., the Brāhmana, the Kṣatriya, the Vaiśya and the śūdra) were created by Me, classifying them according to the Guṇas predominant in each and apportioning corresponding duties to them; though the originator of this creation, know Me, the Immortal Lord, to be a non-doer.
column5: The four orders of society (viz., the Brāhmana, the Kṣatriya, the Vaiśya and the śūdra) were created by Me, classifying them according to the Guṇas predominant in each and apportioning corresponding duties to them; though the originator of this creation, know Me, the Immortal Lord, to be a non-doer.

column3:
na māṃ(ṅ) karmāṇi limpanti, na me karmaphale spṛhā।
iti  māṃ(y̐)  yo'bhijānāti,  karmabhirna  sa  badhyate॥4.14॥
column4: Since I have no craving for the fruit of actions, actions do not taint Me. Even he who thus knows Me in reality is not bound by actions.
column5: Since I have no craving for the fruit of actions, actions do not taint Me. Even he who thus knows Me in reality is not bound by actions.

column3:
evaṃ(ñ) jñātvā kṛtaṃ(ṅ) karma, pūrvairapi mumukṣubhiḥ।
kuru karmaiva tasmāttvaṃ(m), pūrvaiḥ(ph) pūrvataraṃ(ṅ) kṛtam॥4.15॥
column4: Having known thus, action was performed even by the ancient seekers for liberation; therefore, do you also perform actions as have been performed by the ancients from antiquity.
column5: Having known thus, action was performed even by the ancient seekers for liberation; therefore, do you also perform actions as have been performed by the ancients from antiquity.

column3:
kiṃ(ṅ)  karma  kimakarmeti,  kavayo'pyatra  mohitāḥ।
tatte karma pravakṣyāmi, yajjñātvā mokṣyase'śubhāt॥4.16॥
column4: What is action and what is inaction? Even men of intelligence are puzzled over this question. Therefore, I shall expound to you the truth about action, knowing which you will be freed from its evil effects i.e., the shackles of karma.
column5: What is action and what is inaction? Even men of intelligence are puzzled over this question. Therefore, I shall expound to you the truth about action, knowing which you will be freed from its evil effects i.e., the shackles of karma.

column3:
karmaṇo hyapi boddhavyaṃ(m), boddhavyaṃ(ñ) ca vikarmaṇaḥ।
akarmaṇaśca  boddhavyaṃ(ṅ),  gahanā  karmaṇo  gatiḥ॥4.17॥
column4: The truth about action must be known and the truth of inaction also must be known; even so, the truth about prohibited action (Vikarma) must be known. For, mysterious are the ways of action.
column5: The truth about action must be known and the truth of inaction also must be known; even so, the truth about prohibited action (Vikarma) must be known. For, mysterious are the ways of action.

column3:
karmaṇyakarma yaḥ(ph) paśyed, akarmaṇi ca karma yaḥ।
sa buddhimānmanuṣyeṣu, sa yuktaḥ(kh) kṛtsnakarmakṛt॥4.18॥
column4: He who sees inaction in action, and action in inaction, is wise among men; he is a Yogī, who has performed all actions.
column5: He who sees inaction in action, and action in inaction, is wise among men; he is a Yogī, who has performed all actions.

column3:
yasya    sarve   samārambhāḥ(kh),    kāmasaṅkalpavarjitāḥ।	
jñānāgnidagdhakarmāṇaṃ(n), tamāhuḥ(ph)  paṇḍitaṃ(m) budhāḥ॥4.19॥
column4: Even the wise call him a sage, whose undertakings are all free from desire and Sankalpa (thoughts of the world) and whose actions are burnt up by the fire of wisdom.
column5: Even the wise call him a sage, whose undertakings are all free from desire and Sankalpa (thoughts of the world) and whose actions are burnt up by the fire of wisdom.

column3:
tyaktvā karmaphalāsaṅgaṃ(n),  nityatṛpto  nirāśrayaḥ।
karmaṇyabhipravṛtto'pi,    naiva   kiñcitkaroti  saḥ॥4.20॥
column4: He, who, having totally given up attachment to actions and their fruit, no longer depends on anything in the world, and is ever content, does nothing at all, though fully engaged in action.
column5: He, who, having totally given up attachment to actions and their fruit, no longer depends on anything in the world, and is ever content, does nothing at all, though fully engaged in action.

column3:
nirāśīryatacittātmā,           tyaktasarvaparigrahaḥ।
śārīraṃ(ṅ) kevalaṃ(ṅ) karma, kurvannāpnoti kilbiṣam॥4.21॥
column4: Having subdued his mind and body, and having given up all objects of enjoyment, free from craving, he who performs sheer bodily action, does not incur sin.
column5: Having subdued his mind and body, and having given up all objects of enjoyment, free from craving, he who performs sheer bodily action, does not incur sin.

column3:
yadṛcchālābhasantuṣṭo,  dvandvātīto  vimatsaraḥ।
samaḥ(s) siddhāvasiddhau ca, kṛtvāpi na nibadhyate॥4.22॥
column4: The Karmayogī, who is contented with whatever is got unsought, is free from jealousy and has transcended all pairs of opposites like joy and grief, and is balanced in success and failure, is not bound by his action.
column5: The Karmayogī, who is contented with whatever is got unsought, is free from jealousy and has transcended all pairs of opposites like joy and grief, and is balanced in success and failure, is not bound by his action.

column3:
gatasaṅgasya     muktasya,    jñānāvasthitacetasaḥ।
yajñāyācarataḥ(kh)  karma,  samagraṃ(m)  pravilīyate॥4.23॥
column4: All his actions get dissolved entirely, who is free from attachment and has no identification with the body and free from the feeling of mine, whose mind is established in the knowledge of Self and who works merely for the sake of sacrifice.
column5: All his actions get dissolved entirely, who is free from attachment and has no identification with the body and free from the feeling of mine, whose mind is established in the knowledge of Self and who works merely for the sake of sacrifice.

column3:
brahmārpaṇaṃ(m) brahma haviḥ(r), brahmāgnau brahmaṇā hutam।
brahmaiva  tena  gantavyaṃ(m),  brahma  karmasamādhinā॥4.24॥
column4: Brahman is the oblation; Brahman is the melted butter (ghee); by Brahman is the oblation poured into the fire of Brahman; Brahman verily shall be reached by him who always sees Brahman in action.
column5: Brahman is the oblation; Brahman is the melted butter (ghee); by Brahman is the oblation poured into the fire of Brahman; Brahman verily shall be reached by him who always sees Brahman in action.

column3:
daivamevāpare yajñaṃ(y̐), yoginaḥ(ph) paryupāsate।
brahmāgnāvapare   yajñaṃ(y̐),   yajñenaivopajuhvati॥4.25॥
column4: Other Yogīs duly offer sacrifice only in the form of worship to gods, while others perform sacrifice by offering the self by the Self itself in the fire of Brahma.
column5: Other Yogīs duly offer sacrifice only in the form of worship to gods, while others perform sacrifice by offering the self by the Self itself in the fire of Brahma.

column3:
śrotrādīnīndriyāṇyanye,    saṃyamāgniṣu   juhvati।
śabdādīnviṣayānanya,     indriyāgniṣu       juhvati॥4.26॥
column4: Others offer as sacrifice their senses of hearing etc into the fires of self-discipline. Other Yogis, again, offer sound and other objects of perception into the fires of the senses.
column5: Others offer as sacrifice their senses of hearing etc into the fires of self-discipline. Other Yogīs, again, offer sound and other objects of perception into the fires of the senses.

column3:
sarvāṇīndriyakarmāṇi,    prāṇakarmāṇi    cāpare।
ātmasaṃyamayogāgnau,    juhvati    jñānadīpite॥4.27॥
column4: Others sacrifice all the functions of their senses and the functions of the vital airs (Prāṇa) into the fire of Yoga in the shape of self-control, kindled by wisdom.
column5: Others sacrifice all the functions of their senses and the functions of the vital airs (Prāṇa) into the fire of Yoga in the shape of self-control, kindled by wisdom.

column3:
dravyayajñāstapoyajñā,       yogayajñāstathāpare।
svādhyāyajñānayajñāśca,  yatayaḥ(s)  saṃśitavratāḥ॥4.28॥
column4: Some perform sacrifice with material possessions; some offer sacrifice in the shape of austerities; others sacrifice through the practice of Yoga; while some striving souls, observing austere vows, perform sacrifice in the shape of wisdom through the study of sacred texts.
column5: Some perform sacrifice with material possessions; some offer sacrifice in the shape of austerities; others sacrifice through the practice of Yoga; while some striving souls, observing austere vows, perform sacrifice in the shape of wisdom through the study of sacred texts.

column3:
apāne  juhvati  prāṇaṃ(m),   prāṇe'pānaṃ(n)  tathāpare।
prāṇāpānagatī    ruddhvā,     prāṇāyāmaparāyaṇāḥ॥4.29॥
column4: Others offer as sacrifice the outgoing breath in the incoming, and the incoming in the outgoing, restraining the course of the outgoing and the incoming breaths, solely absorbed in the restraint of the breath.
column5: Others offer as sacrifice the outgoing breath in the incoming, and the incoming in the outgoing, restraining the course of the outgoing and the incoming breaths, solely absorbed in the restraint of the breath.

column3:
apare  niyatāhārāḥ(ph),   prāṇānprāṇeṣu juhvati।
sarve'pyete    yajñavido,    yajñakṣapitakalmaṣāḥ॥4.30॥
column4: Other Yogīs offer the act of exhalation into that of inhalation; even so, others the act of inhalation into that of exhalation. There are still others given to the practice of Prāṇāyāma (breath- control), who, having regulated their diet and controlled the processes of exhalation and inhalation both, pour their vital airs into the vital airs themselves. All these have their sins consumed away by sacrifice and understand the meaning of sacrificial worship.
column5: Other Yogīs offer the act of exhalation into that of inhalation; even so, others the act of inhalation into that of exhalation. There are still others given to the practice of Prāṇāyāma (breath- control), who, having regulated their diet and controlled the processes of exhalation and inhalation both, pour their vital airs into the vital airs themselves. All these have their sins consumed away by sacrifice and understand the meaning of sacrificial worship.

column3:
yajñaśiṣṭāmṛtabhujo,   yānti   brahma   sanātanam।
nāyaṃ(l̐) loko'styayajñasya, kuto'nyaḥ(kh) kurusattama॥4.31॥
column4: Arjuna, Yogīs who enjoy the nectar that has been left over after the performance of a sacrifice attain the eternal Brahma. To the man who does not offer sacrifice, even this world is not happy; how, then, can the other world be happy?
column5: Arjuna, Yogīs who enjoy the nectar that has been left over after the performance of a sacrifice attain the eternal Brahma. To the man who does not offer sacrifice, even this world is not happy; how, then, can the other world be happy?

column3:
evaṃ(m)  bahuvidhā  yajñā,  vitatā  brahmaṇo mukhe।
karmajānviddhi tānsarvān, evaṃ(ñ) jñātvā vimokṣyase॥4.32॥
column4: Many such forms of sacrifice have been set forth in detail in the Vedas; know them all as involving the action of mind, senses and body. Thus, knowing the truth about them you shall be freed from the bondage of action (through their performance).
column5: Many such forms of sacrifice have been set forth in detail in the Vedas; know them all as involving the action of mind, senses and body. Thus, knowing the truth about them you shall be freed from the bondage of action (through their performance).

column3:
śreyāndravyamayādyajñāj, jñānayajñaḥ(ph) parantapa।
sarvaṃ(ṅ) karmākhilaṃ(m) pārtha, jñāne parisamāpyate॥4.33॥
column4: Arjuna, sacrifice through Knowledge, is superior to sacrifice performed with material things. For all actions without exception culminate in Knowledge, O son of Kuntī.
column5: Arjuna, sacrifice through Knowledge, is superior to sacrifice performed with material things. For all actions without exception culminate in Knowledge, O son of Kuntī.

column3:
tadviddhi   praṇipātena,   paripraśnena   sevayā।
upadekṣyanti  te  jñānaṃ(ñ),  jñāninastattvadarśinaḥ॥4.34॥
column4: Understand the true nature of that Knowledge by approaching seers of Truth. If you prostrate at their feet, render them service, and question them with an open and guileless heart, those wise seers of Truth will instruct you in that Knowledge.
column5: Understand the true nature of that Knowledge by approaching seers of Truth. If you prostrate at their feet, render them service, and question them with an open and guileless heart, those wise seers of Truth will instruct you in that Knowledge.

column3:
yajjñātvā  na  punarmoham,  evaṃ(y̐)  yāsyasi pāṇḍava।
yena  bhūtānyaśeṣeṇa,  drakṣyasyātmanyatho  mayi॥4.35॥
column4: Arjuna, when you have achieved enlightenment, ignorance will delude you no more. In the light of that knowledge, you will see the entire creation first within your own Self, and then in Me (the Oversoul).
column5: Arjuna, when you have achieved enlightenment, ignorance will delude you no more. In the light of that knowledge, you will see the entire creation first within your own Self, and then in Me (the Oversoul).

column3:
api cedasi  pāpebhyaḥ(s),  sarvebhyaḥ(ph)  pāpakṛttamaḥ।
sarvaṃ(ñ)  jñānaplavenaiva,  vṛjinaṃ(m)  santariṣyasi॥4.36॥
column4: Even if you were the most sinful of all sinners, this Knowledge alone would carry you, like a raft, across all your sins.
column5: Even if you were the most sinful of all sinners, this Knowledge alone would carry you, like a raft, across all your sins.

column3:
yathaidhāṃsi samiddho'gniḥ(r), bhasmasātkurute'rjuna।
jñānāgniḥ(s)  sarvakarmāṇi,  bhasmasātkurute tathā॥4.37॥
column4: For, as the blazing fire reduces the fuel to ashes, Arjuna, even so the fire of Knowledge turns all actions to ashes.
column5: For, as the blazing fire reduces the fuel to ashes, Arjuna, even so the fire of Knowledge turns all actions to ashes.

column3:
na  hi  jñānena  sadṛśaṃ(m),  pavitramiha  vidyate।
tatsvayaṃ(y̐) yogasaṃsiddhaḥ(kh), kālenātmani vindati॥4.38॥
column4: In this world there is no purifier as great as Knowledge; he who has attained purity of heart through prolonged practice of Karmayoga, automatically sees the light of Truth in the self in course of time.
column5: In this world there is no purifier as great as Knowledge; he who has attained purity of heart through prolonged practice of Karmayoga, automatically sees the light of Truth in the self in course of time.

column3:
śraddhāvāl̐labhate jñānaṃ(n), tatparaḥ(s) saṃyatendriyaḥ।
jñānaṃ(l̐) labdhvā parāṃ(m) śāntim, acireṇādhigacchati॥4.39॥
column4: He who has mastered his senses, is exclusively devoted to his practice and is full of faith, attains Knowledge; having had the revelation of Truth, he immediately attains supreme peace in the form of God-realization.
column5: He who has mastered his senses, is exclusively devoted to his practice and is full of faith, attains Knowledge; having had the revelation of Truth, he immediately attains supreme peace in the form of God-realization.

column3:
ajñaścāśraddadhānaśca,    saṃśayātmā   vinaśyati।
nāyaṃ(l̐) loko'sti na paro, na sukhaṃ(m) saṃśayātmanaḥ॥4.40॥
column4: He who lacks discrimination, is devoid of faith, and is at the same time possessed by doubt, is lost to the spiritual path. For the doubting soul there is neither this world nor the world beyond, nor even happiness.
column5: He who lacks discrimination, is devoid of faith, and is at the same time possessed by doubt, is lost to the spiritual path. For the doubting soul there is neither this world nor the world beyond, nor even happiness.

column3:
yogasannyastakarmāṇaṃ(ñ), jñānasañchinnasaṃśayam।
ātmavantaṃ(n) na karmāṇi, nibadhnanti dhanañjaya॥4.41॥
column4: Arjuna, actions do not bind him who has dedicated all his actions to God according to the spirit of Karmayoga, whose doubts have been dispelled by wisdom and who is self-possessed.
column5: Arjuna, actions do not bind him who has dedicated all his actions to God according to the spirit of Karmayoga, whose doubts have been dispelled by wisdom and who is self-possessed.

column3:
tasmādajñānasambhūtaṃ(m), hṛtsthaṃ(ñ) jñānāsinātmanaḥ।
chittvainaṃ(m) saṃśayaṃ(y̐) yogam, ātiṣṭhottiṣṭha bhārata॥4.42॥
column4: Therefore, Arjuna slashing to pieces, with the sword of knowledge, this doubt in your heart, born of ignorance, establish yourself in Karmayoga in the shape of even-mindedness, and stand up for the fight.
column5: Therefore, Arjuna slashing to pieces, with the sword of knowledge, this doubt in your heart, born of ignorance, establish yourself in Karmayoga in the shape of even-mindedness, and stand up for the fight.

column3:
Om tatsaditi śrīmadbhagavadgītāsu upaniṣatsu brahmavidyāyāṃ(y̐)
yogaśāstre śrīkṛṣṇārjunasaṃvāde jñānakarmasannyāsayogo nāma
 caturtho'dhyāyaḥ ||

om śrīparamātmane namaḥ
śrīmadbhagavadgītā
atha pañchamo'dhyāyaḥ

arjuna uvāca
sannyāsaṃ(ṅ) karmaṇāṃ(ṅ) kṛṣṇa, punaryogaṃ(ñ) ca śaṃsasi।
yacchreya  etayorekaṃ(n),  tanme  brūhi  suniścitam॥5.1॥
column4: Arjuna said :Kṛṣņa, you extol Sāṅkhyayoga (the Yoga of Knowledge) and then the Yoga of Action. Pray, tell me which of the two is decidedly conducive to my good.
column5:
